title: Severe Hemorrhage -- Uncontrolled Bleeding
steps: Apply direct pressure with sterile dressing or cleanest available material
If wound is on extremity and direct pressure fails, apply commercial tourniquet 2-3 inches above wound
Note the time of tourniquet application and mark 'TQ' with time on patient forehead or tourniquet
If no tourniquet available, pack wound tightly with gauze and apply maximum pressure
Elevate the bleeding extremity above heart level if possible
Do NOT remove initial dressing -- add additional layers on top
Monitor for signs of shock: pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion
Keep victim warm with blanket or coat
contraindications: Do NOT apply tourniquet to neck, chest, or abdomen
Do NOT remove embedded objects -- stabilize them in place
Do NOT give food or water to victim with severe bleeding
Do NOT apply tourniquet directly over a joint

title: Moderate Hemorrhage -- Controllable Bleeding
steps: Apply firm, direct pressure with sterile gauze or cleanest available cloth
Maintain constant pressure for minimum 10 minutes without checking
If bleeding soaks through, add more material on top -- do NOT remove first layer
Once bleeding slows, apply pressure bandage to maintain compression
Elevate injured area above heart level
Monitor wound for continued bleeding every 5 minutes
Clean wound edges if visible debris, but do not probe wound
Prepare for transport to medical facility
contraindications: Do NOT apply tourniquet for controllable bleeding
Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide on wound (damages tissue)
Do NOT remove bandage to check wound frequently

title: Severe Fracture -- Open or Compound Fracture
steps: Control any bleeding first -- apply pressure around (not on) the fracture site
Do NOT attempt to push bone back into place
Immobilize the fracture by splinting the joint above and below the break
Use rigid material for splinting: board, rolled magazine, or SAM splint
Pad the splint with soft material to prevent pressure sores
Check circulation below the splint every 10 minutes (pulse, color, sensation)
Cover any open wound with sterile moist dressing
Apply cold pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling (20 minutes on, 20 off)
Treat for shock: lay flat, elevate legs if no spinal injury suspected
contraindications: Do NOT move the victim unless in immediate danger
Do NOT attempt to realign the bone
Do NOT splint too tightly -- must maintain circulation
Do NOT apply ice directly to skin
equipment_needed: Roller bandage or tape for securing splint

title: Severe Burns -- Second or Third Degree
steps: Ensure scene safety -- remove heat source or move victim from danger
Stop the burning process: cool with clean, room-temperature water for 20 minutes
Remove clothing and jewelry near the burn unless stuck to skin
Cover burn loosely with sterile non-adhesive dressing or clean cling wrap
Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedies
For chemical burns: brush off dry chemicals first, then irrigate with water
Elevate burned extremities above heart level
Monitor airway -- facial burns or smoke inhalation may cause swelling
Treat for shock: keep warm, lay flat, elevate legs
Calculate burn size: victim palm equals approximately 1% body surface area
contraindications: Do NOT apply ice or ice water -- causes further tissue damage
Do NOT break blisters
Do NOT remove clothing stuck to burn
Do NOT apply adhesive bandages directly to burn
Do NOT use cotton or fluffy material on burns
equipment_needed: Non-adhesive sterile dressings or cling wrap

title: Cardiac Arrest -- No Pulse, Not Breathing
steps: Confirm unresponsiveness: tap shoulders and shout
Call for AED immediately
Place victim on firm, flat surface
Begin chest compressions: center of chest, 2 inches deep, 100-120 per minute
Allow full chest recoil between compressions
If trained: give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions
If untrained: hands-only CPR -- continuous compressions without stopping
If AED arrives: turn on, follow voice prompts, apply pads, stand clear for analysis
Switch compressors every 2 minutes to maintain quality
Continue CPR until professional responders arrive or victim shows signs of life
Do NOT stop CPR to check for pulse -- minimize interruptions
contraindications: Do NOT delay CPR to look for equipment
Do NOT compress over the xiphoid process (bottom of sternum)
Do NOT perform rescue breathing if you suspect toxic gas exposure
Do NOT stop CPR unless victim is clearly reviving or you are physically unable to continue

title: Concussion -- Suspected Traumatic Brain Injury
steps: Keep victim still -- do NOT let them return to activity
Monitor level of consciousness using AVPU scale (Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive)
Check pupils: both should be equal size and reactive to light
Ask orientation questions: name, date, location, what happened
Monitor for danger signs: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, seizures
If victim is conscious, keep them sitting upright to reduce intracranial pressure
Apply cold pack to any external head injury
Do NOT give aspirin or blood-thinning medications
If victim becomes unconscious, place in recovery position and monitor airway
Document the time of injury and any changes in mental status
contraindications: Do NOT allow victim to sleep unsupervised for 24 hours
Do NOT give aspirin or ibuprofen (may increase bleeding risk)
Do NOT move victim if spinal injury is suspected
Do NOT apply direct pressure to a skull fracture

title: Choking -- Complete Airway Obstruction
steps: Ask the victim: 'Are you choking?' -- if they cannot speak, cough, or breathe, act immediately
Stand behind the victim and wrap arms around their waist
Make a fist with one hand and place it just above the navel
Grasp your fist with the other hand
Deliver quick, upward abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver)
Continue thrusts until object is expelled or victim becomes unconscious
If victim becomes unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR, check mouth for object before breaths
For pregnant or obese victims: perform chest thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts
For infants under 1 year: 5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts
contraindications: Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps
Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants
Do NOT slap adult victim on back while standing (may worsen obstruction)

title: Hypothermia -- Core Temperature Below 95F / 35C
steps: Move victim to warm, dry environment if possible
Remove any wet clothing -- cut off if necessary
Wrap victim in dry blankets, sleeping bag, or emergency blanket -- insulate from ground
Cover the head to prevent heat loss
Apply warm packs to neck, armpits, and groin (major blood vessels)
If conscious and able to swallow: give warm, sweet drinks (NO alcohol or caffeine)
Handle victim gently -- rough movement can trigger cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia
Monitor breathing and pulse -- be prepared for CPR
If victim is unconscious, place in recovery position
Rewarm gradually -- do NOT use hot water or direct heat
contraindications: Do NOT give alcohol -- it dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss
Do NOT rub or massage extremities
Do NOT use direct heat sources (fire, heating pad) on skin
Do NOT place victim in hot water
equipment_needed: Warm packs or hot water bottles (wrapped in cloth)

title: Suspected Spinal Cord Injury
steps: Do NOT move the victim unless in immediate life-threatening danger
Stabilize the head and neck in the position found -- use hands on both sides
Instruct the victim to remain completely still
Assess sensation and movement: ask victim to wiggle fingers and toes
Check for numbness, tingling, or inability to move extremities
If victim must be moved, use log-roll technique with minimum 3 rescuers
Maintain manual in-line stabilization throughout any movement
Apply cervical collar if available and trained to do so
Monitor airway -- jaw thrust without head tilt if breathing support needed
Treat other injuries while maintaining spinal immobilization
contraindications: Do NOT flex, extend, or rotate the spine
Do NOT sit the victim up
Do NOT remove helmet unless airway access required
Do NOT use head-tilt chin-lift for airway -- use jaw thrust only
equipment_needed: Towels or blankets rolled for lateral stabilization

title: Anaphylaxis -- Severe Allergic Reaction
steps: Identify signs: hives, facial swelling, throat tightness, difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure
If victim has an epinephrine auto-injector: assist with administration to outer thigh
Hold auto-injector in place for 10 seconds
Position victim: sitting up if having difficulty breathing, flat if showing signs of shock
If both breathing difficulty AND shock: position at 45 degrees (compromise position)
Monitor airway continuously -- be prepared for swelling to worsen
If no improvement in 5-15 minutes, a second dose of epinephrine may be needed
Begin CPR if victim becomes unresponsive and stops breathing
Note time of epinephrine administration for medical team
Keep victim warm and calm
contraindications: Do NOT delay epinephrine administration if available
Do NOT give oral medications if airway is swelling
Do NOT leave victim alone -- rapid deterioration possible
Do NOT force victim to lie flat if they are struggling to breathe

title: Active Seizure Management
steps: Note the time the seizure started
Clear the area around the victim of hard or sharp objects
Place something soft under the head if possible
Do NOT restrain the victim or hold them down
Do NOT put anything in the victim's mouth
Roll victim onto their side (recovery position) once convulsions stop
Check airway and breathing after seizure ends
Stay with the victim until they are fully conscious and oriented
If seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes, this is a medical emergency (status epilepticus)
Document seizure duration and type of movements for medical responders
contraindications: Do NOT restrain the victim during a seizure
Do NOT put objects in the mouth -- they cannot swallow their tongue
Do NOT give food or water until fully alert
Do NOT attempt to move the victim during active seizure unless in danger

title: Smoke Inhalation Injury
steps: Remove victim from smoke-filled environment to fresh air immediately
Assess breathing: look for soot around nose/mouth, singed nasal hair, hoarse voice
Administer high-flow oxygen if available (15 L/min via non-rebreather mask)
Position victim sitting upright to ease breathing
Monitor for delayed airway swelling -- can occur hours after exposure
If victim is not breathing, begin rescue breathing with barrier device
Watch for stridor (high-pitched breathing sound) indicating airway narrowing
Remove contaminated clothing to prevent continued chemical absorption
Keep victim warm and calm
Be prepared for rapid deterioration -- airway can swell shut quickly
contraindications: Do NOT re-enter smoke-filled area without proper SCBA
Do NOT give mouth-to-mouth without barrier device (carbon monoxide transfer risk)
Do NOT assume victim is fine -- delayed symptoms are common
Do NOT give water if victim is having difficulty swallowing
